author: nftprof
raw_text:
ERC-8170 has been officially assigned — thank you @abcoathup for the review and number assignment!

Updates since submission:
- Posted a detailed response on the ERC-6551 Magicians thread clarifying how ERC-8170 complements (not competes with) ERC-6551, ERC-8004, and ERC-7857
- Live contracts deployed on Pentagon Chain (Chain ID 3344)
- AINFTRegistry v2 supports both native minting and binding to existing ERC-721s
- Companion proposal ERC-6551A (PR #1559) provides the lightweight registry for existing collections

Next steps:
- Dogfooding: minting ERC-8170 AINFTs for our own production agents
- Seeking reviewer feedback on clone() lineage tracking (mandatory vs optional)
- Cross-standard composability with ERC-8004 and ERC-7857

Full source and docs: https://github.com/blockchainsuperheroes/ai-core/tree/main/EIPs

author: chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]
raw_text:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Use required `eip` preamble metadata**

The preamble uses `erc: <to be assigned>` instead of the required numeric `eip` field, which makes this draft fail the repository’s validator configuration (`config/eipw.toml` enforces `preamble-req`, `preamble-eip`, and `preamble-file-name`). As written, CI will reject this proposal before content review, so the metadata format needs to be corrected for the document to be processable.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

author: chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]
raw_text:
**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Accept variable-length liveness attestations**

`submitLivenessProof` constrains `attestation` to `bytes32`, but the spec text says this value is a TEE attestation or signature; real SGX/Nitro attestation payloads and standard signatures are larger than 32 bytes. This forces implementations to truncate or hash proofs, which makes on-chain/off-chain verification behavior ambiguous and conflicts with the liveness-proof requirements described later in the spec.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

author: Copilot
raw_text:
The Ouroboros setup steps transfer the NFT into its own TBA *before* configuring an executor. Once `ownerOf(tokenId)` is the TBA itself, an implementation that gates `setExecutor` to the token owner cannot be called externally, so the draft needs to specify an authorization/bootstrapping rule (or reorder steps to set executors before self-transfer).
```suggestion
3. **Configure Executor**: Grant signing permissions to the agent's TEE-held key while the NFT is still held by an externally controlled address.
4. **Transfer**: Transfer Token `N` to its own TBA address

Implementations that gate executor configuration to `ownerOf(tokenId)` MUST perform executor configuration (step 3) before self-transfer (step 4), or otherwise define an equivalent bootstrap authorization rule, to avoid making executor setup unreachable once the TBA owns the token.

After step 4, the ownership graph becomes:
```

author: Copilot
raw_text:
The permission bitmap defines `EXECUTE_CALL`, `EXECUTE_DELEGATECALL`, and `TRANSFER_ASSETS`, but the standard does not specify any callable entrypoints (on the NFT contract or the TBA) that consume these flags, nor how permission checks are performed. As written, different implementations could interpret these bits incompatibly; the spec should define the required execution/asset-transfer functions (or remove/relax the unused permission bits).
```suggestion
| 0 | Reserved | Implementation-specific execution control (not standardized by this ERC) |
| 1 | Reserved | Implementation-specific execution control (not standardized by this ERC) |
| 2 | `ANCHOR_STATE` | Can update the state anchor |
| 3 | `MANAGE_EXECUTORS` | Can add/remove other executors |
| 4 | Reserved | Implementation-specific asset management (not standardized by this ERC) |
| 5 | `SUBMIT_LIVENESS` | Can submit liveness proofs |
| 6-255 | Reserved | For future standardization or implementation-specific extensions |

This ERC only standardizes the semantics of `ANCHOR_STATE`, `MANAGE_EXECUTORS`, and `SUBMIT_LIVENESS`. Any use of the reserved bits (including execution and asset-transfer behavior) and the corresponding entrypoints and permission checks on the NFT contract or TBA is explicitly left to implementers and is out of scope for this standard.
```

author: Copilot
raw_text: This section claims `anchorState` supports both “State Anchor” and “Action Anchor” patterns, but the on-chain interface/event naming (`StateAnchored`, `anchorState`, `stateHash/stateUri`) remains state-specific, making action anchoring ambiguous for indexers and implementers. Consider standardizing action anchoring explicitly (e.g., separate `anchorAction`/`ActionAnchored`, or an `anchorType` parameter) so consumers can reliably distinguish the two.

author: Copilot
raw_text:
Test 2 uses `execute(...)` on the TBA as the primary authorization surface, but earlier the draft states executors should call the identity contract directly (not through `TBA.execute()`) due to the self-ownership circular dependency. This is a spec inconsistency; update the tests (and/or the earlier requirement) so there is one clear, consistent executor invocation path.
```suggestion
When: 0xTEE calls execute(0xTarget, 0, calldata) on the self-sovereign agent's identity contract
Then: The call is executed successfully
When: 0xUnauthorized calls execute(0xTarget, 0, calldata) on the self-sovereign agent's identity contract
```

author: Copilot
raw_text:
The “Source Code” section points readers to a `contracts/` directory and specific Solidity files, but this repository does not contain that directory/files. Either add the referenced source under the repo (preferred for a spec PR) or update this section to link to an existing, stable location for the reference implementation source.
```suggestion
A reference implementation of this standard includes:

- A `SelfSovereignAgentNFT` contract that implements executor permissions and state anchoring as specified above.
- An `ISelfSovereignAgent` interface that matches the interface definitions in this document.
```

author: Copilot
raw_text:
Front-matter uses `erc:` but other ERC docs in this repo use `eip:` (e.g., `ERCS/erc-8004.md:1-12`). Using a different key can break the site/build tooling that expects the standard `eip` metadata field; align this header with the existing convention (and ensure the filename follows the repo’s `erc-<number>.md` pattern once an ID is assigned).
```suggestion
eip: <to be assigned>
```

author: Copilot
raw_text:
`requires` should include any EIPs referenced in the Specification section per the repo template (`erc-template.md:10`). This draft references ERC-7857 in “State Anchoring” as a suggested access-control mechanism; consider adding `7857` here (and any other spec-level EIP dependencies you cite).
```suggestion
requires: 165, 721, 6551, 7857
```

Learn how to get started.

author: abcoathup
raw_text:
```suggestion
This proposal defines an optional extension to [ERC-8004](./eip-8004.md) that introduces a delayed metadata update mechanism for agent identity records. The extension prevents immediate mutation of agent metadata after verification by introducing a pending state and activation delay.
```

author: abcoathup
raw_text:
```suggestion
[ERC-8004](./eip-8004.md) allows agents to update metadata instantly via URI changes. While this provides flexibility, it also creates a risk where an agent can pass verification and then immediately swap its endpoint or configuration.
```

author: abcoathup
raw_text:
```suggestion
The proposal introduces minimal additional storage and events. It does not mandate a specific cooldown duration or update policy. This preserves [ERC-8004](./eip-8004.md)'s flexibility while providing stronger guarantees for ecosystems requiring higher trust signals.
```

author: abcoathup
raw_text:
```suggestion
This extension is fully optional. Contracts not implementing it remain compliant with [ERC-8004](./eip-8004.md). Clients SHOULD monitor new events when available but MUST remain compatible with legacy behavior.
```

raw_text: @Cyberpaisa you need to fix the validator issues (references to ERC-8004) and then wait for an ERC editor to review (which I am not).

author: 0xRowan
raw_text:
Hi @SamWilsn @xinbenlv @g11tech,

This PR is now ready for formal review. All previous feedback has been addressed and reference implementations are complete.

### 📋 Summary

This PR introduces two related ERC standards:
- **ERC-8086**: Privacy Token - A minimal interface for native privacy-preserving fungible tokens
- **ERC-8085**: Dual-Mode Fungible Tokens - Tokens supporting both ERC-20 and privacy modes with seamless conversion

### 🔗 Live Deployments

Both standards have functional testnet implementations:

**ERC-8085 (Dual-Mode Token)**
- Test App: https://testdmt.zkprotocol.xyz/
- Factory: [0x64EeF485F82918bBf61dd5349300F5a84f907140](https://sepolia.basescan.org/address/0x64EeF485F82918bBf61dd5349300F5a84f907140#code)
- Implementation: [0xd8714b3E2B490585c22d5a7a030f3946e46940c4](https://sepolia.basescan.org/address/0xd8714b3E2B490585c22d5a7a030f3946e46940c4#code)

All contracts are deployed on Base Sepolia (Chain ID: 84532) and fully verified.

### 💬 Community Discussion

Active discussions on Ethereum Magicians:
- [ERC-8086 Discussion](https://ethereum-magicians.org/t/erc-8086-privacy-token/26623)
- [ERC-8085 Discussion](https://ethereum-magicians.org/t/erc-8085-dual-mode-fungible-tokens/26592)

### 🎯 Key Design Decisions

1. **ERC-8086 as Foundation**: Provides reusable privacy primitives (commitments, nullifiers, Merkle trees)
2. **ERC-8085 Builds on Top**: Combines ERC-20 + ERC-8086 for dual-mode functionality
3. **Dual-Layer Merkle Tree**: 16-level active subtree + 20-level finalized tree (68.7B note capacity)
4. **Full ERC-20 Compatibility**: ERC-8085 is backward compatible with existing DeFi infrastructure
5. **Application-Layer Solution**: Deployable today without protocol changes

### Request

Please let me know if you need any clarification, additional documentation, or changes to move this proposal forward. I'm happy to address any feedback promptly.

Thank you for your time and consideration!

author: SamWilsn
raw_text:
````suggestion
1. Hold Note A (100 privacy balance)
2. Call toPublic(50) with proof sending output to attacker's own privacy address
3. If contract skips BURN_ADDRESS check:
   - Attacker receives 50 public balance (converted from privacy mode)
   - Note B (50) sent to attacker's privacy address ← Still spendable in privacy mode!
   - Note C (50 change)
   Result: 50 + 50 + 50 = 150 (created 50 out of thin air!)
````

This isn't code.

author: SamWilsn
raw_text: Requirements, even security ones, should be in the Specification section, though you should highlight and discuss them in this section.

author: SamWilsn
raw_text: We're a little more lenient on external links in assets, but this is a bit much. Don't include external links please!

author: SamWilsn
raw_text:
If we remove the stuff that's implied for most ERCs:

```suggestion
description: Native privacy-preserving fungible tokens
```

we're left mostly with a restatement of the title. You've got ~140 characters to play with, so use them to get into the meat of your proposal.

raw_text:
```suggestion
To enable **client interoperability** across different implementations, this standard defines an **OPTIONAL but RECOMMENDED** Privacy Configuration File mechanism, inspired by [ERC-8004](./eip-8004.md)'s Agent Registration File pattern.
```

Link to the markdown files please.

author: douglasborthwick-crypto
raw_text:
The Reputation / Attestation Interop section names ERC-8004 as the hook's trust source — specifically, `beforeAction` hooks that gate `setProvider` on a reputation threshold. Worth flagging a production API that already produces the kind of signed attestation output a hook would consume.

[InsumerAPI](https://insumermodel.com/developers/api-reference/) issues ECDSA-signed boolean attestations across 32 chains (30 EVM + Solana + XRPL) — token balances, NFT ownership, EAS attestations, Farcaster identity. A reputation-gated hook can call `POST /v1/attest` before allowing a job to proceed and get back a verifiable credential without the hook needing its own RPC connections:

```json
{
  "attestation": {
    "id": "att_...",
    "pass": true,
    "results": [{
      "met": true,
      "conditionHash": "0xabc...",
      "blockNumber": "0x14d3a00"
    }],
    "attestedAt": "2026-03-09T..."
  },
  "sig": "MEUCIQDa...",
  "kid": "insumer-attest-v1"
}
```

The signature is ES256 (P-256), verifiable via JWKS at `GET /v1/jwks` ([RFC 7517](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7517)). `conditionHash` is a SHA-256 of the canonical condition JSON, anchored to a specific block — so the hook can verify exactly what was checked. Optional `format: "jwt"` returns a standard ES256 JWT that any off-the-shelf verifier (Kong, Cloudflare Access, AWS API Gateway) can validate against the same JWKS endpoint.

Free tier covers 100 attestations/day. Self-serve key at [insumermodel.com/developers](https://insumermodel.com/developers/api-reference/).

[OpenAPI spec](https://insumermodel.com/openapi.yaml) · [MCP server](https://www.npmjs.com/package/mcp-server-insumer) · [LangChain tools](https://pypi.org/project/langchain-insumer/)
